data.history: The research reveals a complex pattern of the Al Jaberi name appearing across different cultural and geographical contexts, but does not establish a clear connection between these various Jaberi lineages and the UAE-based Al Jaberi family. The research shows Iranian Jaberis like Mirza Salman Jaberi Isfahani, a 16th-century Safavid grand vizier from Isfahan whose family served the Aq Qoyunlu and claimed descent from the Persian Sufi poet Khvajeh Abdollah Ansari. There are also contemporary Iranian figures like model Mahlagha Jaberi from Isfahan, and Moroccan philosopher Mohammed Abed al-Jabri. Within the UAE context, the research mentions several Al Jaberi individuals associated with the Dhawahir tribe, including footballers Rayan and Rami Yaslam Al Jaberi, actor Ali bin Swelah Al Jaberi, and footballer Waleed Salem Sulaiman Al Jaberi, who are listed as members of the Al Jaberi subsection within the Dhawahir tribal confederation. However, the research does not provide specific historical details about how this particular Al Jaberi lineage within the UAE developed, their migration patterns, or their business activities beyond their existing connection to the Bani Yas confederation and Liwa Oasis region.
data.description: The Al Jaberi are documented as both a subsection within the UAE's Dhawahir tribal confederation and as a family lineage within the Bani Yas confederation, though the research does not clarify the relationship between these two contexts. Contemporary Al Jaberi individuals in the UAE are primarily known through their involvement in sports and entertainment.
data.originStory: Origins uncertain - the Al Jaberi name appears across multiple unconnected contexts including Iranian aristocratic families descended from Persian Sufi poets, and as a subsection within the UAE's Dhawahir tribe. The connection between these various Jaberi lineages remains unclear from available research.

data.history: The Al Qasimi (Arabic: القاسمي, plural: القواسم/Al Qawasem) are a Huwala Arab dynasty of Hashemite origin, descended from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province. During the 18th century, they established themselves as rulers in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, with Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi ruling from 1722-1747. The Qawasem embraced the Wahhabi movement and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they emerged as a dominant maritime power controlling both shores of the Persian Gulf, with territories in Ras Al Khaimah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their control of Gulf trade routes led to conflicts with Oman and Britain, resulting in British military campaigns in 1809 and 1819. The British labeled them 'pirates,' though current ruler Sultan bin Mohammed Al Qasimi contested this characterization in his book 'The Myth of Arab Piracy in the Gulf.' After the 1820 General Maritime Treaty, the family adapted to the new political reality, eventually becoming founding members of the UAE in 1971, with the family splitting to rule both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah as separate emirates. In 1869, Ras Al Khaimah became fully independent from neighbouring Sharjah, though it was re-incorporated from September 1900 to July 1921. The family has experienced internal succession disputes, including the 1972 coup in Sharjah when Sheikh Khalid bin Mohammed Al Qasimi was killed by forces loyal to Saqr bin Sultan Al Qasimi, leading to Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi taking power.
data.description: The Al Qasimi are the ruling family of both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates in the UAE, descended from Huwala Arabs with Hashemite lineage. They transformed from a dominant 18th-19th century maritime power controlling Gulf trade routes to modern rulers focused on education, culture, and economic development.
data.originStory: The Qawasim tribe from which the dynasty originates are Huwala Arabs of Hashemite origin, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province before establishing power in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah during the 18th century.
data.modernStatus: Current rulers: Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi (Sharjah) and Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi (Ras Al Khaimah). The Sharjah branch focuses heavily on education and culture, with Sultan founding the American University of Sharjah, University of Sharjah, and numerous cultural institutions. The family has diversified into various business sectors including real estate, manufacturing, pharmaceuticals (Julphar), and tourism development.

data.history:
The Al-Alawi are descendants of the Ba'Alawi Sadat (Sādat Bā 'Alawiy), a distinguished group of Hadhrami Sayyid families originating in Hadhramaut in the southwest corner of the Arabian Peninsula. They trace their lineage through Ahmad al-Muhajir, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 CE (318H), and claim descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib via al-Husayn ibn Ali. The Ba'Alawi name derives from Alawi ibn 'Ubaydillah ibn Ahmad al-Muhajir, the first of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi and the only one to produce a continued line in Hadhramaut. Classical Islamic scholars including Ibn Hajar al-Haytami, Yusuf al-Nabhani, and Murtada al-Zabidi have validated their genealogy, and their lineages are preserved in detailed records including the famous 15-volume genealogy ledger by Abd al-Rahman al-Mashhur.

The Ba'Alawi follow the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence and Ash'ari creed, and established their own Sufi order, the Al-Baalawi Tariqah. Their founder Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi, known as 'Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam,' symbolically broke his sword and announced that the Alawi way would be non-violent, renouncing any tariqa that uses violence. The family became prominent religious scholars and merchants in Hadhramaut, establishing themselves at the top of the social ladder through their esteemed lineage, significant contributions, and influence in spreading Islamic principles.

Beginning around 1700 CE, the Ba'Alawi began migrating extensively across the Islamic world for da'wah (missionary work), reaching Southeast Asia, East Africa, the Gulf region, and beyond. In the Gulf, they became prominent as religious authorities and scholars. Some members held positions as Naqib al-Ashraf in Mecca and Medina in recent centuries, with many Ba'Alawi families being well-known to these institutions and having their genealogies officially recorded.

The family includes notable contemporary figures like Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki (1944-2004), one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholars of modern times from Saudi Arabia. Born into the respected Maliki family of Mecca, which had produced scholars who taught in the Haram for centuries, he served as a teacher at the Sacred Mosque in Mecca and was considered the Mujaddid of the 20th-21st century. His family had five ancestors who served as Maliki Imams of the Haram of Mecca. Despite some modern challenges to their lineage through DNA analysis showing haplogroup G rather than the expected J1, the Ba'Alawi genealogy continues to be validated by contemporary Islamic authorities and genealogists.
data.description: The Al-Alawi are a noble Sayyid family descended from the Ba'Alawi Sadat of Hadhramaut, claiming lineage from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib. They are established as prominent religious scholars and have spread throughout the Islamic world since 1700 CE, with significant presence in the Gulf region as religious authorities.
data.originStory: The Al-Alawi trace their origins to Ahmad al-Muhajir, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 CE (318H). They claim descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib via al-Husayn ibn Ali. Their name derives from Alawi ibn 'Ubaydillah ibn Ahmad al-Muhajir, the first of al-Muhajir's descendants named 'Alawi and the only one to establish a continued lineage in Hadhramaut.
data.modernStatus: Established as religious scholars and authorities across the Islamic world, particularly in the Gulf region. Notable contemporary presence includes the Maliki family of Mecca, which has produced prominent Islamic scholars including Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki (1944-2004).

data.history: The Al-Qassab family shows connections to the broader Shia community in the Persian Gulf region, with historical records indicating their presence in Bahrain by the early 19th century. According to research on Bahraini religious practices, 'Al Qassab' is listed among the oldest and most distinguished matams (Shia congregation halls) in the Fareej el-Makharqa quarter of central Manama, constructed at the turn of the 19th century alongside other prominent establishments like Matam al-Ajam al-Kabeer and Matam Bin Rajab. This suggests the family had established religious and community leadership roles in Bahrain's Shia community by the early 1800s. The name Al-Qassab, meaning 'the butcher' in Arabic, aligns with their traditional occupation in livestock trading and meat commerce. Genetic studies of Bahraini populations indicate that some families with similar names show affinity to the J2 haplogroup, which is associated with Iranian, Caucasian, and Mesopotamian ancestry rather than central Arabian Peninsula origins. Additionally, the research mentions a Syrian footballer named Jihad Qassab (1975-2016), indicating the family name extends beyond the Gulf region. A notable modern family member, Zaid Al-Qassab, serves as Global CEO of M+C Saatchi Group as of May 2024, having previously held the position of CMO at Channel 4, representing the family's evolution into international business leadership while maintaining their Gulf connections.
data.description: The Al-Qassab are a family with historical roots in Bahrain's Shia community, traditionally involved in livestock trading and meat commerce, who have evolved into modern business leadership while maintaining their religious and cultural connections.

data.history:
The Dulafid dynasty was an Arab dynasty that served as governors of Jibal (western Iran) for the Abbasid caliphs during the 9th century CE. The family belonged to the Arab tribe of Banu Ijl, who participated in the vanguard of the Muslim conquest of Iraq in the 7th century. The dynasty's documented history begins with two brothers, Idris and Isa, sons of Ma'qel, who were traders residing in Kufa during the Umayyad period (724-743 CE). They were imprisoned by Umayyad authorities, possibly for supporting the Abbasid cause or due to a trade dispute. After accumulating wealth, Idris moved to the Zagros region and purchased land near Hamadan, while his son Isa moved to Isfahan with his sons before the family eventually settled in Karaj, where they acquired extensive cultivated lands, palaces, and fortresses by the 9th century.

The first Dulafid to achieve political prominence was Abu Dulaf al-Ijli, son of Isa, who was appointed governor of Jibal by Caliph Harun al-Rashid (786-809 CE). Despite siding with al-Amin against al-Ma'mun in the Abbasid civil war and holding Shi'ite beliefs, Abu Dulaf was pardoned and retained his position due to his reputation for learning, competence, and integrity. He maintained excellent relations with Caliph al-Mu'tasim (832-842 CE), serving as both military commander against the Khurramites and governor, even becoming the Caliph's drinking companion before dying in Baghdad in 839/40 CE. Abu Dulaf's long rule firmly established the family's hereditary control over their domains, known as 'al-Igharayn' (the two fiefs), with rights to coin money, collect tribute, and rule almost independently except for annual tribute to Baghdad.

The dynasty continued under Abu Dulaf's son Abd al-Aziz (c. 840-874 CE), followed by Dulaf ibn Abd al-Aziz (874-879 CE) and then Ahmad ibn Abd al-Aziz (879-893 CE). During the weakening of Abbasid central authority in the 860s known as the 'Anarchy at Samarra,' the Dulafids acted increasingly as independent rulers. Ahmad had a complex relationship with both the Abbasids and the rising Saffarid power, switching allegiances multiple times and being appointed to governorships of Fars and Kirman while dealing heavy defeats to Saffarid forces in 886 CE. However, after Ahmad's death in 893 CE, Caliph al-Mu'tadid moved swiftly to reassert caliphal control by intervening in succession disputes between Ahmad's sons Bakr and Umar, dividing Dulafid territories in 894 CE and finally deposing them entirely in 896 CE.

The dynasty met its violent end during the Abbasid campaign to reincorporate Jibal into direct caliphal control. The last Dulafid ruler, Abu Layla al-Harith ibn Abd al-Aziz, was killed in battle against the Abbasid governor Isa al-Nushari on January 26, 898 CE, approximately 12 kilometers from Isfahan. According to al-Tabari's account, an arrow pierced his throat during the battle, causing his forces to flee. His head was subsequently sent to Baghdad, arriving on March 3, 898 CE, marking the definitive end of the Dulafid dynasty after over a century of rule in western Iran.
data.description: The Dulafid dynasty was a 9th-century Arab ruling family from the Banu Ijl tribe that governed the Jibal region of western Iran as increasingly independent governors under the Abbasid Caliphate until their final defeat and elimination by Abbasid forces in 896-898 CE.
data.originStory: The Dulafids originated from the Arab tribe of Banu Ijl, who participated in the Muslim conquest of Iraq. The family's rise began with trader brothers Idris and Isa, sons of Ma'qel, who resided in Kufa during the Umayyad period (724-743 CE) before eventually settling in the Zagros region and accumulating land and wealth around Karaj and Isfahan.

data.history: The research provided contains limited information about the Rouben family, primarily documenting two distinct individuals: Rouben Mamoulian (1897-1987), a prominent Armenian-American film director, and Rouben Rouben, a contemporary figure operating an electronics business in Bahrain. Rouben Mamoulian was born in Tiflis (modern Tbilisi, Georgia) to an Armenian family, with his mother Virginie (née Kalantarian) coming from a family of wealthy landowners and financiers who directed the Armenian theatre, while his father Zachary served as a bank president. The family was raised in the Armenian Apostolic faith and held sympathies with the Czarist regime. Due to ethnic violence following the Russian Revolution of 1905, the Mamoulian family moved to Paris for three years before eventually fleeing Russia during the 1917 revolution. Rouben Mamoulian later immigrated to the United States, becoming a naturalized citizen in 1930 and achieving significant success in American theater and cinema. He directed acclaimed Broadway productions including Oklahoma! (1943) and Carousel (1945), and became a pioneer in early sound cinema and Technicolor filmmaking. His 1929 film 'Applause' was noted among the works that brought greater fluidity to early talkies and experimented with expressive use of sound. The research also mentions Rouben Rouben, a contemporary member of Bahrain's small Jewish community who operates an electronics business, though no direct family connection to the Mamoulian branch is established in the provided materials.

data.history: The Al Tayer family is a prominent Dubai merchant family that established themselves as successful business leaders in the UAE. The modern Al Tayer Group was founded in 1979 as a privately held holding company, marking the beginning of their transformation into one of the UAE's largest business conglomerates. The family's business empire expanded significantly under the leadership of Obaid Humaid Al Tayer, who served as UAE Finance Minister and became chairman of the Al Tayer Group. Al Tayer holds a bachelor's degree in electrical engineering from the University of Colorado and served in various government positions including as a member of the Federal National Council from 1981 to 1985, Dubai Municipality Council from 1982 to 1990, and Dubai Chamber of Commerce and Industry board from 1982 to 1991. In 1984, Obaid Al Tayer, along with partners Abdullah Al Rostamani and Juma Al Majid, purchased Gulf News and formed Al Nisr Publishing, transforming it into one of the region's leading English-language newspapers. The Al Tayer Group diversified into multiple sectors including automotive representation (Land Rover, Jaguar, Ford, Lincoln, Ferrari, and Maserati since 1982), luxury retail through Al Tayer Insignia operating nearly 200 stores across 6 Middle Eastern countries representing brands like Bulgari, Yves Saint Laurent, Prada, and Armani, real estate, engineering, and media. The family's retail division achieved notable success by opening the first Bloomingdale's store outside the United States in Dubai Mall in 2010, and launching the luxury e-commerce platform Ounass.com in 2016. From 2000 to 2008, Obaid Al Tayer served as chairman of Dubai Chamber of Commerce and Industry and was a member of Dubai Economic Council from 2004 to 2008. In November 2019, he was appointed chairman of Etisalat Group, and served as Minister of State for Financial Affairs until September 2021. Currently employing nearly 9,000 people, the Al Tayer Group represents one of the UAE's most diversified business conglomerates, with operations spanning automotive, retail, real estate, engineering, and cinema exhibitions across the GCC region.
data.description: The Al Tayer family is a prominent Dubai merchant family that built one of the UAE's largest business conglomerates, the Al Tayer Group, with interests spanning automotive, luxury retail, real estate, and media across the GCC region.
data.modernStatus: Currently operates the Al Tayer Group, a privately held holding company established in 1979 employing nearly 9,000 people across 6 countries. The group operates nearly 200 retail stores, 23 automotive showrooms, represents major luxury brands and automotive manufacturers, and has partnerships in cinema operations across the GCC. Obaid Humaid Al Tayer serves as chairman and was formerly UAE Minister of State for Financial Affairs until 2021.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information specifically about the Al Azzawi family as a GCC merchant family. While the documents reference several individuals with the surname Al-Azzawi or variations thereof, they are primarily Iraqi figures rather than Gulf merchant family members. The documents mention Fadhil al-Azzawi, an Iraqi poet and novelist born in Kirkuk in 1940, and Hikmat Mizban Ibrahim al-Azzawi (1933-2012), an Iraqi politician who served as Deputy Prime Minister and twice Minister of Finance under Saddam Hussein's government. There are also references to 'Abu Azzawi' who led Chaouia tribes in Morocco in resistance against French occupation during the early 1900s, and Dia al-Azzawi (born 1939), an Iraqi artist. data.history: The Juma Al Majid family represents one of the UAE's most successful merchant dynasties, with their modern prominence centered around Juma al Majid (born 1930 in Al Shandagah, Dubai). Born as the son of a pearl diver within the traditional maritime economy of the Trucial States, Juma transformed his family's fortunes through entrepreneurial acumen and strategic business expansion. Starting at age 15 working in his uncle Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair's shop in Deira, he established his own business in 1950, initially selling fabric before pivoting to air conditioners. His partnership with Mohammad Al Qaz, a trader importing goods from Kuwait and Bahrain, proved transformative as they expanded into cross-Gulf trade, taking tobacco and Omani dry lime to Kuwait and Bahrain while returning with Swiss and French fabric and watches. The family's business empire grew systematically through the decades, with Juma securing exclusive distribution agreements for international brands like Yokohama Tires (1958), General Electric appliances, and later Samsung, Hyundai, and Hitachi. By the 1970s, his company had become the second largest distributor of General Electric products, selling up to 700,000 air conditioners annually. The 1971 UAE Federation marked further expansion as he partnered with Al Qaz to open a Pepsi factory in Dubai and diversified into automotive, real estate, construction, hospitality (managing Taj Palace Hotel), and heavy equipment. Beyond commerce, Juma Al Majid established himself as a significant cultural and educational philanthropist, founding the Islamic and Arabic Studies College (1987), the Juma Al Majid Center for Culture and Heritage (1989), and the National Charity Schools (1983), while serving in key economic positions including Chairman of Dubai Economic Council and Vice Chairman of UAE Central Bank. By 2016, Al Majid was ranked among the richest Arabs in the world, having built a diversified conglomerate with interests spanning trading, manufacturing, real estate, travel and tourism, hospitality, investments, automobiles, contracting and commercial operations across the UAE and GCC.
data.description: The Juma Al Majid family is a prominent Emirati merchant family known for their extensive trading operations and diversified business empire spanning over seven decades. Founded by Juma al Majid in 1950, they successfully transitioned from traditional cross-Gulf trade to modern commercial enterprises, building one of the most reputed organizations in the Middle East. The family is also recognized for significant contributions to education, culture, and heritage preservation in the UAE.
data.originStory: The Al Majid family emerged from humble beginnings within Dubai's traditional pearl diving and maritime trade economy. The family's commercial transformation began when Juma started working at age 15 in his uncle Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair's shop in Deira, eventually establishing his own business in 1950 and partnering with Mohammad Al Qaz to expand into cross-Gulf trading operations.
data.modernStatus: Juma Al Majid Holding Group, established in 1950, is a major Emirati conglomerate with over 35 companies and 150+ branches across UAE and GCC. The group operates in automotive (Kia, Hyundai Genesis), real estate development, contracting, FMCG distribution (exclusive UAE distributor for SC Johnson brands), travel, retail, hospitality (manages Taj Palace Hotel), and various other sectors. Juma al Majid was ranked among the richest Arabs in the world as of 2016.

data.history: The Al Muhaidib family traces its origins to the Tamim tribe and settled in Buraidah, Qassim during the 18th century, establishing themselves as successful merchants specializing in date trading and livestock commerce. The modern business empire began in 1946 when Abdul Kadir bin Abdul Muhsin Al-Muhaidib founded the first company under the name Abdulatif and Abdulkadir Al-Muhaidib Company, initially focusing on wholesale building materials and foodstuffs. The company underwent several name changes reflecting its growth - becoming Al-Muhaidib and Al-Nafea Company in 1959, then A.K Al-Muhaidib & Sons Group of Companies in 1979. The group expanded significantly into food distribution starting with rice marketing in 1959, eventually establishing the Giant Stores retail chain which later merged with Panda Retail Company. Their building materials division grew to serve Saudi Arabia's economic and industrial development, with companies like Masdar Technical Supplies and Masdar Building Materials operating over 70 branches with more than 1 million square meters of storage capacity and over 1,300 employees. The family's business interests diversified into multiple sectors including financial services through SABB bank and Gulf Union insurance, real estate development through Al Oula Development and Rafal Real Estate, contracting through Thabat (formerly Al-Muhaidib Contracting) with over 14,000 employees, and energy through ACWA Power founded in 2002. The current chairman is Sulaiman A.K. Al-Muhaidib, and a notable family member, Ibrahim al-Muhaidib, served as president of Al-Nassr Football Club until his resignation in 2024. The Al Muhaidib Group has also been involved in major construction projects including King Abdullah Sports City Stadium in Jeddah, built through a joint venture between Al Muhaidib Contracting Company and Belgian construction company BESIX Group.
data.description: The Al Muhaidib family is a prominent Saudi merchant dynasty from the Qassim region that has built one of Saudi Arabia's largest diversified business groups. Their empire spans food distribution, building materials, financial services, real estate, contracting, and energy sectors through multiple major companies.

data.history: The Al Kharafi family is one of Kuwait's most prominent merchant dynasties, descended from the Utub tribal confederation that migrated from Najd to the Arabian Gulf coast in the early 1700s. They were part of Kuwait's founding merchant community alongside other Utub families who settled in what became modern Kuwait around the mid-1700s. The family's modern business empire was established when Mohamed Abdul Mohsen Al Kharafi founded M.A. Kharafi & Sons in 1956 as a contracting company. Under the leadership of his sons, particularly Nasser Al-Kharafi, the company expanded dramatically into a major conglomerate with $4.3 billion in sales by 2011, spanning construction, telecommunications, food services, and investments across more than 30 countries. Nasser Al-Kharafi became one of the world's wealthiest individuals with a net worth of $10.4 billion as of 2011, ranking as the 77th richest man globally. The family has wielded significant political influence through Jassem Al-Kharafi, who served as Speaker of Kuwait's National Assembly for five terms from 1999-2012, the longest tenure in Kuwait's parliamentary history. Jassem played a critical role during Kuwait's 2006 constitutional crisis, coordinating the peaceful transition of power to Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah when the incumbent emir was deemed unfit to rule due to illness. However, the family faced controversy in 2013 when allies of Sheikh Ahmad Al-Fahad claimed to possess tapes showing Jassem Al-Kharafi and Sheikh Nasser Al-Mohammed discussing plans to topple the Kuwaiti government, though these coup allegations were later retracted and deemed fabricated. The family has also made significant contributions to academia through Dr. Faiza Al-Kharafi, who became the first woman to head a major university in the Middle East when she served as President of Kuwait University from 1993-2002. Today, the third generation continues the family's influence with Bader Al-Kharafi serving as Vice Chairman and Group CEO of Zain Group, one of the Middle East's largest telecommunications companies.

They rose from 18th-century founding merchant community status to become major business leaders through the M.A. Kharafi & Sons conglomerate, while also wielding significant political influence in Kuwait's National Assembly.

The family previously held major stakes in National Bank of Kuwait, Americana restaurant chain, and various other holdings.

data.history: The Al Amoudi family traces its origins to Sheikh Sa'eed ibn Isa al-Amoudi (1203/1204-1272 CE), a prominent Sunni Shafi'i Islamic scholar from Qaydun in Hadhramaut's Wadi Daw'an region. Sheikh Sa'eed's lineage traces back through his ancestor Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Bakri, known as 'Amoududdin' (Pillar of the religion), from whom the family name derives, ultimately connecting to the first Muslim Caliph Abu Bakr As-Siddiq through a documented genealogical chain. His father, Sheikh Isa ibn Ahmad Al-Amoudi, was originally a Mufti of Medina who migrated to spread Islamic learning, eventually settling in Hadhramaut where he married into the Bani Muhammad of the Himyarite Siban tribe. Sheikh Sa'eed became a renowned Sufi master, founding the Tariqah Al-Amoudiyyah and allegedly receiving the Staff of Moses and Coat of Joseph from his teacher Muhammad ibn Ali Ba'Alawi. His descendants established the independent Al-Amoudi state in Wadi Daw'an from 1531-1899 CE, with their capital at Budha village, before falling to the Al-Quaiti Sultanate. The family's modern international prominence emerged through Mohammed Hussein Ali Al Amoudi, born in Dessie, Ethiopia in 1946 to a Yemeni father from the Mashaikh Al-Amoudi tribe and an Ethiopian mother from Wollo Province. He migrated to Saudi Arabia with his brother Mauricet, became a Saudi citizen, and built a business empire worth $9.3 billion through his conglomerate holding companies MIDROC and Corral Petroleum Holdings. His empire spans construction, oil refineries, mining, agriculture, and manufacturing across Saudi Arabia, Ethiopia, Sweden, and Morocco, making him Ethiopia's richest man and the second richest Saudi Arabian citizen globally. Al Amoudi was arrested in November 2017 during Crown Prince Mohammad bin Salman's anti-corruption crackdown and held for 14 months before his release in January 2019.
data.description: A Hadhrami family descended from medieval Islamic scholar Sheikh Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi, tracing lineage to Caliph Abu Bakr. Modern prominence through billionaire businessman Mohammed Hussein Al Amoudi's multinational empire worth $9.3 billion spanning construction, oil, mining, and agriculture.

folkLegends.title: The Staff of Moses and Coat of Joseph
folkLegends.story: Abu Madyan Shu'ayb of Morocco allegedly possessed the Staff of Moses and Coat of Joseph, stipulating in his will before his 1198 CE death that they be sent to Muhammad ibn Ali Ba'Alawi in Hadhramaut. After Abu Madyan's death, the artifacts were transported via Iraq and Mecca to Tarim, where Muhammad ibn Ali Ba'Alawi received them and later gave them to Sheikh Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi as an honor. The staff is described as three cubits tall with a sweet smell, whitish brass tip tinged with silver, and black smudge marks. The alleged coat remains in a yellow satin bundle inside green velvet, never opened by the Al-Amoudi family. These artifacts remain in the custodianship of Sheikh Mutahar Abood Al-Amoudi in Budha village.
folkLegends.source: Hadhrami oral tradition and historical accounts

data.history:
The Abunayyan family is a prominent Saudi Arabian family whose rise to international prominence is closely tied to their connection with the Jordanian royal family. The family's most notable figure is Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bin Suleiman Abunayyan, whose daughter Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan became a member of the Jordanian royal family through her marriage to Prince Hashim bin Hussein on April 15, 2006. This union connected the Abunayyan family to the Hashemite dynasty of Jordan, with Prince Hashim being the younger son of the late King Hussein and Queen Noor of Jordan. The marriage has produced five children: Princess Haalah (born 2007), Princess Rayet Al-Noor (born 2008), Princess Fatima Al-Alia (born 2011), Prince Hussein Haidara (born 2015), and Prince Mohammad Al-Hassan (born 2019), cementing the family's ties to the Hashemite royal lineage.

The Abunayyan family's social standing is further reinforced through their maternal connections to other influential Saudi families. Princess Fahdah's maternal grandfather, Sheikh Turki bin Khaled bin Ahmed Al Sudairi, served as head of the Saudi Human Rights Commission, indicating the family's connections to the powerful Al Sudairi lineage, one of the most prominent tribal confederations in Saudi Arabia. This connection to the Al Sudairi family, which has produced numerous high-ranking officials and maintains close ties to the Saudi royal family, demonstrates the Abunayyan family's integration into the upper echelons of Saudi society and their access to significant political and social networks within the Kingdom.

The family's prominence has been solidified through their participation in high-profile royal events and their continued presence in international diplomatic circles. Princess Fahdah attended the wedding of Crown Prince Hussein of Jordan and Rajwa Al Saif on June 1, 2023, highlighting the family's ongoing involvement in regional royal affairs. The strategic nature of the marriage alliance between the Abunayyan and Hashemite families reflects the broader patterns of inter-Gulf and regional elite marriages that serve to strengthen diplomatic, economic, and political ties between ruling families and prominent merchant dynasties across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant.
data.description: A prominent Saudi Arabian family known internationally through Princess Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan's marriage to Prince Hashim bin Hussein of Jordan in 2006, connecting them to the Jordanian royal family. The family maintains significant social standing in Saudi Arabia through connections to influential families including the Al Sudairi.

The research covers several unrelated individuals with the surname Al-Saleh or variations thereof, but these do not appear to constitute a coherent family structure. The individuals mentioned include Saleh al-Arouri (1966–2024), a Palestinian Hamas leader born in 'Arura near Ramallah; Ali Abdullah Saleh (1947–2017), former President of Yemen from the Sanhan clan; Muhammad ibn Saleh al-Uthaymin (1929–2001), a Saudi Islamic scholar from the Banu Tamim tribe; and various other Saudi religious scholars including contemporary figures like Saleh al-Fawzan and Saleh al-Maghamsi. The research also mentions Saleh and Daoud Al-Kuwaity, Kuwait-born Israeli musicians of Babylonian and Persian Jewish ancestry who were prominent in Iraqi and Kuwaiti music in the early 20th century, and Ahmed Ali Abdullah Saleh, son of the former Yemeni president. None of these individuals or their families appear to be connected to each other or to represent a unified Al Saleh family lineage that would constitute a coherent GCC family history. The surname 'Saleh' appears to be simply a common Arabic name meaning 'righteous' or 'pious,' rather than indicating membership in a specific family or tribal group.
data.description: No verifiable unified Al Saleh family exists in the research provided. The surname appears among various unrelated individuals across different countries and contexts.
data.nameEtymology: Saleh is an Arabic name meaning 'righteous' or 'pious,' derived from the Semitic root and related to the Hebrew names Shelah, Selah or Methuselah

data.history: The Al Humaidi family is documented across multiple regions of the GCC and broader Middle East, with notable members in Kuwait, Qatar, Yemen, and Libya. The most prominent contemporary figure is Abdulla Al-Humaidi, a Kuwaiti businessman born in January 1986 who pursued medical studies at the Royal College of Surgeons in Dublin before obtaining an MBA from the University of Leicester. Despite his medical training, he entered business, establishing Kuwaiti European Holdings (KEH) as an investment firm operating across real estate, healthcare, and leisure sectors, with significant expansion into the UK during the early 2010s. His major ventures included acquiring Ebbsfleet United F.C. in 2013 through KEH Sports Ltd and launching the ambitious £3.5 billion London Resort theme park project in 2014, though both enterprises faced significant difficulties. The London Resort project ultimately collapsed into insolvency in 2023, leading to Abdulla Al-Humaidi's bankruptcy declaration in November 2023 and subsequent legal proceedings. In Qatar, Lance Corporal Bader Saad Mohammed Al Humaidi Al Dosari served as a security official who was killed in the September 9, 2025 Israeli airstrike on Doha. The family name also appears in Kuwaiti politics, with Bader Al-Humaidi mentioned as a parliamentary candidate in the December 2020 elections where 40 MPs announced their intention to vote for him as speaker of parliament, though he ultimately lost to Marzouq Al-Ghanim. Additional family members include Yemeni judoka Salah Al-Humaidi who competed in the 1992 Summer Olympics, and historically, Al-Khweldi Muhammad Salih Abdullah El-Hamedi, a Libyan major general who was part of Gaddafi's inner circle and founding member of the Libyan Revolutionary Command Council.
data.description: A family name documented across multiple GCC states with notable members in business, politics, and military service, most prominently represented by Kuwaiti businessman Abdulla Al-Humaidi and his troubled business empire.
data.modernStatus: Mixed fortunes - Abdulla Al-Humaidi's business empire collapsed with his 2023 bankruptcy following the failure of the £3.5 billion London Resort project, though family members maintain involvement in Ebbsfleet United F.C. and the £1 billion Northfleet Harbourside development project

data.history: The Al Zawawi family represents multiple distinct lineages across different regions, with the most prominent being the Omani Al Zawawi family. The Omani branch was established by Abdul-Mun'im Al-Zawawi, whose son Omar Zawawi built the Zawawi Mosque in Muscat in 1985 to commemorate his father's death. This mosque is notable for having the entire Qur'an engraved on metal plates on its walls, replacing an earlier 1906 structure that was demolished in 2005. The family achieved significant political prominence through Qais Bin Abdul Munim Al Zawawi, who served as a minister and was tragically killed in a 1995 car accident with Sultan Qaboos bin Said in Salalah. Beyond Oman, separate Al Zawawi individuals have held prominent positions across the region. Palestinian diplomat Salah Al-Zawawi (1937-2023) was born in Safed during the British Mandate and migrated to Syria with his family during the 1948 Palestine War, settling in Aleppo. He became a founding member of the Fatah movement alongside Yasser Arafat and served as Palestine's ambassador to Iran for over 40 years, from 1981 to 2022, earning the title 'Sheikh Al-Sofara' (Sheikh of Ambassadors). His daughter Salam al-Zawawi succeeded him as Palestinian ambassador to Iran. The research also references historical connections to North African Berber regions, particularly the Zawawi (Zouaoua) tribes mentioned in medieval Arabic sources discussing the Hafsid dynasties of Béjaïa, though the relationship between these various Al Zawawi lineages remains unclear.
data.description: The Al Zawawi represents multiple distinct family lines across the Gulf, Levant, and North Africa, with the most prominent being an influential Omani family known for religious and political contributions, including mosque construction and high-level government service.
data.originStory: Origins disputed - the name appears across multiple unrelated lineages. The Omani branch traces to Abdul-Mun'im Al-Zawawi, while Palestinian members trace to Safed. Historical references suggest possible connections to North African Berber Zawawi (Zouaoua) tribes, but relationships between these various lineages are unclear.
data.modernStatus: The Omani Al Zawawi family maintains religious and political influence through mosque endowments and government service. Palestinian branch continues diplomatic service with Salam al-Zawawi serving as Palestine's ambassador to Iran since 2022.

data.history: The research provides extremely limited information about the Abu Karaki tribe of the Arabian Peninsula. The only potential connection found relates to the Mahamid tribe in Jordan, where 'the Abu Karaki family' is mentioned as 'the Mahamid who came from Daraa, immigrated from them to Umm al-Fahm, after which to the Karak governorate.' However, this reference appears to be about a Jordanian family branch rather than the Arabian Peninsula tribal group known as Abu Karaki. The research does not provide any verified information about the Abu Karaki tribe's genealogical origins, whether they trace to Adnani or Qahtani lineage, their sub-tribal divisions, or their historical development within the Arabian Peninsula. No information is available about their founding ancestor Karak, their migration patterns, territorial control, or relationships with other Gulf tribes. The research also provides no details about their role in modern Gulf affairs, political alignments, or historical significance. Without additional documented sources specifically addressing this Arabian Peninsula tribal branch, their exact origins, genealogical connections, and historical narrative remain undetermined and cannot be accurately reconstructed from the available research.

Al'ahmad represents one of the documented Palestinian branches that emerged from the broader Mahamid tribal matrix, according to genealogical records tracing their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before undertaking extensive migrations across the Arabian Peninsula. When the tribe grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, they made a collective decision to migrate northward rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb tribal clans who shared common ancestry through fifth-generation grandfathers.

The Mahamid migration took them first to Dumat Al-Jandal in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region in present-day Jordan, where they established themselves in such numbers that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the area. The Mahamid formed alliances with local Karak clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan.

The Mahamid rule in Karak lasted for an extended period, though historical accounts suggest their governance became harsh over time, prompting different local clans to unite against them. This eventually led to the decline of Mahamid political control in the region. Through subsequent dispersals and migrations, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the Levant, with different sub-groups settling in Syria, Jordan, and Palestine. Al'ahmad specifically emerged as one of the Palestinian branches during these later dispersals, representing the continuation of the Mahamid lineage in Palestine alongside other documented branches including kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, and al'asead.

folkLegends.title: The Great Consultation
folkLegends.story: When the Mahamid tribe grew too large for their territories in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz, the tribal elders gathered to decide their future. Some suggested conquering neighboring tribes, but this was rejected because most neighbors were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe, united by shared ancestry through their fifth grandfather. Instead, they chose the path of migration, demonstrating wisdom over warfare.

The Al Bu Falah represents a small but influential subsection of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, historically numbering only 15-20 households according to J.G. Lorimer. Also known as the Al Nahyan, this clan has traditionally supplied the paramount shaykh of the Bani Yas and has served as the ruling family of Abu Dhabi since 1793. The Al Bu Falah is part of the House of Al Falahi (Āl Bū Falāḥ), which belongs to the broader Bani Yas tribal confederation of Najdi origin that traces its lineage to Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. The Bani Yas confederation settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700, with the Al Bu Falah holding the most power within the confederation at that time.

The Al Bu Falah owned extensive date gardens and residences across seven villages in the Liwa region, though most members did not reside there permanently. During winter months, they lived in the desert with their camels, while in summer many participated in pearling expeditions aboard vessels owned by other sections of the Bani Yas. The Al Bu Falah were notably the first among the Bani Yas to acquire property in the Buraimi Oasis, a practice systematically continued by members of the ruling family. Much of the land formerly associated with the Al Bu Falah village of Muwaiji is now held by heirs of Muhammad bin Khalifah (d. 1979), with additional holdings belonging to Shaikha Latifah bint Zayid (d. 1979) and other ruling family members.

The dynasty's founding is attributed to Sheikh Dhiyab ibn Isa Al Nahyan, who ruled the Bani Yas from 1761 to 1793. In 1761, Dhiyab sent a hunting party from Liwa that tracked a gazelle to a brackish spring on an island, which became Abu Dhabi (literally 'Father of the Gazelle'). In 1793, he ordered his son Shakbut to move to the island and establish a settlement of about 20 houses and a fort. The village expanded rapidly to 400 houses within two years, becoming the new Bani Yas capital. Dhiyab's rule ended tragically in 1793 when his cousin Hazza bin Zayed bin Muhammad bin Falah killed him during an argument. The Bani Yas elders supported Dhiyab's son Shakhbut as successor, establishing the Al Bu Falah dynasty that continues to rule Abu Dhabi today.

The family developed close relationships with other tribal groups, particularly the Al Bu Shamis section of the Na'im tribe. This alliance may have originated from assistance provided by Shaikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut in helping the Al Bu Shamis regain control of Qabil and Sunaynah in Dhahirah prior to 1833. The Al Bu Shamis, although belonging to the Ghafiri faction like the rest of the Na'im, developed a close alliance with the Al Bu Falah despite traditional rivalries. Today, the House of Al Nahyan, as the ruling branch of the Al Bu Falah, controls Abu Dhabi and manages sovereign wealth funds including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and Mubadala Investment Company, with an estimated US$1 trillion worth of assets under management, making them the second richest family globally as of 2024.

data.history: The Halalmah (Al Halami) are documented as one of the branches within the Bani Yas tribal confederation, according to J.C. Lorimer's early 20th century Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf. They were identified by Lorimer as a distinct group that was already in the process of integration into the larger Bani Yas confederation and are now generally regarded as a minor subsection of the Bani Yas. Like other Bani Yas branches, they would have been part of the broader tribal movements and settlements associated with the confederation, which had its origins in the Najd region of Central Arabia and descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. The Halalmah's integration into the Bani Yas structure reflects the fluid nature of tribal affiliations in the region, where smaller groups often became incorporated into larger confederations for political and economic advantages. Their current status as a recognized branch of the Bani Yas places them within the broader tribal framework that spans the UAE and parts of Qatar.

data.history: Al Katheer is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribal confederation, belonging specifically to the Ahl al-Buwait division alongside Al-Suwait, Al-Sa'id, and Bani Hussein branches. The Al-Dhafeer tribe descends from Tayy and traces its genealogical origins to Banu Lam from the Arabs of the Hejaz. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes can be traced back to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina. The Al-Dhafeer confederation was historically known as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their distinctive raiding practices that included women and children, and their enthusiasm for victories. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding other tribes and migration, as they did not maintain permanent settlements for much of their early history. Al Katheer, as part of this confederation, participated in the nomadic lifestyle of the central Arabian deserts, living primarily in the Desert of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), which encompasses the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe played significant military roles in regional conflicts, including the 1782 Battle of Zubarah where Dhafeer cavalry helped the House of Khalifa end Persian control in Bahrain. In 1924, during the Battle of Qaysar in southern Iraq, the Dhafeer tribe successfully defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe, killing 72 fighters including commander Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish. Around 1926, after Emir Ibn Saud's conquest of much of Arabia, the Saudi branch of the tribe was allowed to settle in several permanent settlements after regional roads were secured. Historical records indicate that Al Katheer, along with other branches of Bani Lam including the Dhafir tribe, settled in areas such as Al-Rass in Al-Qassim Province after earlier Arab tribes like Bani Asad had departed the region. Today, most Al Katheer descendants live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, holding positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors and engineers, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain and Turkey.

The Al Murrah (Arabic: آل مرة) are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe, which belongs to the Qahtanite branch of Arabian tribes, specifically the group known as Banu Hamdan, making them native to southwestern Arabia. The Banu Yam was the progenitor tribe of both the Al Murrah and the 'Ujman of eastern Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Gulf coast. Unlike their parent tribe Yam, whose majority follows the Sulaymani Isma'ili branch of Shi'ite Islam, the Al Murrah branches are Sunni Muslims.

Historically, the Al Murrah were camel-herding nomads who controlled and travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, including the formidable Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali). They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals, with seven clans making up the Saudi branch of the tribe. Their nomadic lifestyle was characterized by extensive migrations, traveling as much as 3,000 kilometres annually. The tribe maintains a presence across multiple Gulf states including Qatar, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Bahrain.

Historically, the Al Murrah constituted a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population and played significant roles in the country's political affairs. In 1885, internal tribal divisions led to a military confrontation when some Al Murrah tribespeople, along with members from the Bani Hajer tribe, renounced their allegiance to Qatar and joined with the hostile Ajman tribe. A regiment of 1,200 partisans composed of loyal Al Murrah and Bani Hajer branches engaged the rebel coalition of 2,000 men, inflicting heavy losses despite being outnumbered.

The tribe's modern political involvement in Qatar proved tumultuous during the late 20th and early 21st centuries. They were strong supporters of Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir who was deposed in 1995 by his son. Eight months after this coup, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996 but failed. In February 2000, 19 alleged perpetrators were sentenced to death and 33 to life imprisonment, though none of the death sentences were executed. Political tensions continued into the 21st century: in 2017, Qatar revoked citizenship of the sheikh and 54 tribespeople, and Al Murrah members were barred from the 2021 Qatari general election, leading to protests. The Al Murrah maintain friendly relations with UAE and Saudi Arabia while opposing the current Iranian regime.

data.history:
The Al Qawasim (القواسم) are a tribal confederation of Hashemite origin, tracing their lineage through Husayn ibn Ali to their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim are classified as Huwala, maintaining ancestral connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining their rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. The confederation's specific historical origins trace to figures like Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi (1722-1747) and subsequent rulers who established their dominance in the northern Emirates region.

During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, becoming close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they had emerged as a formidable maritime power controlling both southern shores at Ras Al Khaimah and northern Persian Gulf territories including Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their confederation was composed of Sunni tribes in the southeastern Gulf region surrounding the cities of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, and they faced strong rivalry with the Omani empire for naval domination along the Persian Gulf.

The Qawasim's control of trade routes in the Persian Gulf led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire over border disputes, religious differences, and naval supremacy. Beginning in 1804, increased Qawasim naval attacks on trading vessels led the British to label them as 'pirates,' resulting in two major military campaigns - the 1809 Persian Gulf campaign and the more successful 1819 expedition under William Keir Grant. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty forced the Qawasim to cease their maritime activities and carry a distinctive peace flag with a red rectangle contained within a white border.

Following the treaties, the Al Qawasim evolved into the ruling dynasties of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, becoming founding members of the United Arab Emirates in 1971. The confederation also maintained extensive commercial and military activities beyond the Persian Gulf, with ties extending to the Gulf of Aden and as far west as Mocha on the Red Sea. They had numerous commercial ties with Somalis and regularly attended trade fairs in Berbera and Zeila. In the 1830s, the Isaaq Sultan Farah Guled and Haji Ali requested military assistance from Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah for joint religious war against the British. Today, branches of Al Qawasim continue to rule as the Al Qasimi families in Ras Al Khaimah under Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi and in Sharjah under Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.

The research focuses entirely on Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah al-Khatib of Morocco (1710-1790) and the historical office of Sharif of Mecca. While the Moroccan research mentions that one of the sultan's daughters, Lalla Lubabah, married 'Sharif Surur' in 1768, this appears to refer to a member of the Hashemite nobility in the Hejaz region rather than the Al-Surur family/clan of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. The Aljeayisa are documented as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid Arab tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The parent Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, establishing themselves as part of the broader Qahtani Arab genealogical framework. When the Mahamid population expanded and their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient to support their growing numbers, the tribe convened to determine their course of action. Rather than engaging in conflict with neighboring tribes—many of whom were related clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe—they collectively decided to migrate northward in search of new settlements. The Mahamid migration route took them first to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region, where they established a significant presence and formed alliances with local clans, most notably the Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. The Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region, with leadership primarily from the Al-Amru clan, though their rule eventually faced opposition from other regional groups. Through these historical migrations and settlements, the Mahamid eventually branched into multiple sub-groups across different regions, with the Aljeayisa emerging as one of the Palestinian branches alongside other groups including the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Almusaeada, Alhasasana, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad.

data.history:
The al-'Amasib represent a complex case of tribal identity spanning multiple regions, with documented presence both in the Arabian Peninsula and Northern Sudan. According to the research, the Sudanese al-'Amasib appear in historical records as one of the riverine clans of the Manasir tribe, specifically among the Manasir al-Nil who inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Dar al-Manasir. Like other Manasir clans, they claim Qahtanite origins through various genealogical traditions, with some tracing lineage to Mansur bin Qahtan of the southern Arabian Qahtaniin confederation.

The Qahtanite connection places al-'Amasib within the broader framework of South Arabian tribal genealogy. The Qahtanites, also known as Banu Qahtan or al-Arab al-Ariba, originate from modern-day Hadhramaut in Yemen and are considered by Arab tradition to be among the original Arabs. According to traditional genealogy, all Qahtanite tribes trace their ancestry through Saba' and his two main branches: Himyar (representing settled southern Arabs) and Kahlan (including both settled and nomadic groups). The Kahlan division encompasses major tribal confederations including Azd, Hamdan, Madhhij, Tayy, and others.

Modern scholarship suggests that the current Manasir community, including the Sudanese al-'Amasib, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian populations, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted common genealogical frameworks for mutual recognition and resource claims. As riverine people along the Nile, al-'Amasib would have pursued small-scale agriculture, particularly date cultivation, while maintaining traditional tribal social structures characteristic of Sudanese riverine communities.

The relationship between the documented Sudanese al-'Amasib and any Arabian Peninsula groups bearing the same name remains unestablished in available research, though both claim Qahtanite origins. The broader Qahtanite tribal movement included significant pre-Islamic migrations, with the Ghassanids representing the last major pre-Islamic Semitic migration from Yemen around 250 CE, settling in the Hauran region and eventually spreading to Palestine and Jordan. After the rise of Islam, Qahtanite tribes became involved in Arab conquests between the 7th and 14th centuries, migrating to newly conquered territories and intermingling with local populations, while engaging in the prolonged Qays-Yaman rivalry that continued until the 19th century.

data.history:
The Awamir is a large Bedouin Arab tribe that originated in the steppes north of the Hadhramut and migrated northward over a 500-year period to settle across the Arabian Peninsula from Muscat and Nizwa to Abu Dhabi and Liwa. By the early 20th century, they numbered approximately 10,000, with 3,500 being nomadic Bedouin and 4,000 settled outside of Oman proper. The Awamir were closely affiliated with the Bani Yas confederation of Abu Dhabi, frequently supporting them in conflicts while maintaining their reputation as warlike, fiercely independent camel breeders and raiders. A subsection called 'Afar was linked to Dhafrah. The tribe was characterized as reputed brave and warlike but also crafty, treacherous and predatory, roaming across the whole Oman peninsula.

In 1848, the Awamir played a crucial role in helping Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan expel Wahhabi forces from Buraimi Oasis, forming part of his tribal confederacy that achieved decisive strategic victory by 1850. They continued supporting Abu Dhabi's rulers through the 1880s wars with Qatar under Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. During the 1930s economic depression following the decline of pearling, increased Awamir raiding activities led to conflicts including the 1946 war between Dubai and Abu Dhabi, forcing British intervention.

Internal tribal conflicts with the Duru in the 1940s caused a schism when headman Salim bin Hamad bin Rakkad led part of the tribe to Al Hasa in 1943, with this group eventually becoming Saudi citizens while the remainder stayed at Buraimi under Abu Dhabi protection. By the 1950s, about 50 Awamir families had acquired date plantations in Buraimi, and by 1968, a census identified 1,721 members, many having transitioned from nomadic life to employment with oil companies, marking the end of their traditional lifestyle. The Manasir were noted as fighting against the Duru, Bani Qitab and Awamir in Buraimi in 1923, indicating inter-tribal conflicts in the region.

The Bani Hadiyah (Arabic: بني هدية) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and northern Oman, constituting one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, alongside the Bani Shatair. The tribe is further subdivided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. The Bani Hadiyah inhabit the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains, with their territory spanning the border between the UAE and northern Oman.

Traditionally, the Bani Hadiyah practiced a seasonal migration pattern that defined their economic and social life. During the pearling season, they would relocate to work in Ras Al Khaimah, particularly in the coastal villages of Sha'am and Rams. In winter, they would return to their mountain strongholds in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains, where they engaged in farming activities. This dual economic system allowed them to participate in both the lucrative pearl trade and mountain agriculture.

The relationship between the Shihuh tribes, including the Bani Hadiyah, and the rulers of Ras Al Khaimah was historically fractious and marked by political tensions. Both Rams and Sha'am, villages where the Bani Hadiyah worked during pearling season, experienced multiple secessions from Ras Al Khaimah rule. These political disputes reflected the independent nature of the Shihuh tribes and their resistance to centralized authority.

The political status of Bani Hadiyah territories was finally resolved in 1921, when the villages of Sha'am and Rams definitively became part of the recognized Trucial State of Ras Al Khaimah. This incorporation marked the end of the recurring cycles of secession and represented the formal integration of these traditionally Shihuh-inhabited areas into the emerging political structure of what would eventually become the United Arab Emirates.

data.history: Based on the extensive research provided, no specific information about 'Bani Muhammad' as a distinct tribal entity was found in the historical sources. The research focuses extensively on other major Arabian tribes including Bani Khalid, Banu Hanifa, Banu Qurayza, and various other tribal confederations, but contains no historical details, genealogical information, or specific events related to any tribe specifically called 'Bani Muhammad'. The sources document detailed tribal histories spanning from pre-Islamic times through the modern period, including the Bani Khalid Emirate (1669-1796) in eastern Arabia, the Banu Hanifa's role in early Islamic history and the Ridda Wars, and various other tribal movements and political activities across the Arabian Peninsula. However, none of these comprehensive tribal histories reference or connect to any group specifically designated as 'Bani Muhammad'. Without concrete historical evidence in the provided research about this particular tribal designation's origins, migrations, political activities, or genealogical connections, the specific identity and historical trajectory of any tribe called 'Bani Muhammad' remains undocumented in these sources. The name 'Bani Muhammad' could theoretically refer to multiple distinct groups across different regions who might trace lineage to an ancestor named Muhammad, but no such specific groups are identified or discussed in the available historical documentation.

Banu Al-Harith traces its origins to Al-Harith ibn Ka'b, establishing the tribe within the Qahtani lineage of southern Arabia. The tribe historically governed multiple cities including Najran, Taif, and areas of what is now southern Saudi Arabia, with their capital at Al-Khubah near Najran. During pre-Islamic times, a significant portion of Banu Al-Harith converted to Judaism, working primarily in goldsmithing and arms repair, and wearing the traditional jambiya. They formed strategic alliances with Banu Madh'hij to successfully conquer Najran, establishing themselves as the dominant power ruling the city for centuries alongside Banu Hamdan. This period of dominance ended during the Christian invasion of Najran. In 523 CE, the Himyarite king Dhu Nuwas, who had converted to Judaism, massacred Christians in Najran, an event that involved the Jewish Banu Al-Harith community. Following these upheavals, sub-clans of the tribe dispersed - one group emigrated to the Dhank region of Oman, while another moved south and founded the district of Bani Al Harith in Sanaa. After the rise of Islam, Banu Al-Harith was included in Point 31 of the Constitution of Medina, being honored as allies to the Muslims while retaining their Jewish religion and receiving the same rights as Banu Awf. The small remnants of the Jewish Banu Al-Harith continued living semi-autonomously in Najran until the 1930s when the Saudi-Yemeni War resulted in Saudi conquest of the city in 1934. Under persecution from governor Amir Turki bin Mahdi, who gave them one day to convert to Islam or evacuate, the remaining Jewish Banu Al-Harith fled south to Sanaa and Aden. Their descendants became part of the small Yemenite Jewish population, most of whom eventually migrated to Israel. The research also reveals connections to other Banu Al-Harith branches through marriage alliances, such as Maymuna bint al-Harith's marriage to Prophet Muhammad in 627, and the tribe's inclusion in various Meccan political factions during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods.

data.history: Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr was a tribal branch within the Quraysh confederation, tracing their lineage to al-Harith, son of Fihr ibn Malik (fl. c. 230-240 CE). According to the Islamic genealogical tradition documented in the research, Fihr ibn Malik is positioned eleven generations before Prophet Muhammad in the Adnani lineage, making this branch part of the broader Quraysh tribal structure that descended from Kinana. The tribe was based in the Hijaz region around Mecca and participated in the complex tribal politics of pre-Islamic Arabia. The genealogical records show that Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr maintained significant political and social integration within the broader Quraysh confederation through strategic marriage alliances. A notable example is the marriage of Al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib, one of Muhammad's uncles, to Ghaziyya bint Qays from Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr, which produced five children including Nawfal, Rabi'ah, Abu Sufyan (Mughirah), Abd Allah, and Arwa. The detailed lineage shows that Ghaziyya was the daughter of Qays ibn Tareef ibn Abd al-Uzza ibn Amir ibn Umayra ibn Wadi'a bin al-Harith ibn Fihr, establishing a clear genealogical connection back to the tribal eponym. Like other Quraysh branches, they were involved in trade and the management of Meccan affairs during the pre-Islamic period. Their historical significance is primarily tied to their role as marriage partners and political allies within the Quraysh confederation, with their tribal identity eventually absorbed into broader formations during the early Islamic period as the traditional tribal structure gave way to the expanding Islamic state.

data.history:
The Banu Khazraj were a major South Arabian Qahtanite tribe that migrated north from Yemen to Medina (then called Yathrib) around the 2nd century CE, alongside their cousin tribe the Banu Aws. According to Abu Muhammad Al-Hasan Ibn Ahmad Al-Hamdani, this migration was part of the Pre-Islamic Exodus of Yemen caused by damage to the Great Marib Dam. The tribe settled in Medina and became one of the two dominant Arab tribes there, but developed a hostile relationship with the Aws that culminated in the Battle of Bu'ath (Battle of Yawm al-Bu'ath) shortly before Muhammad's arrival. During this conflict, the Banu Khazraj allied with the Jewish tribe Banu Qaynuqa, while their rivals the Aws fought alongside the Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza. The Khazraj were defeated in this battle.

When Muhammad migrated to Medina in 622 CE, most of the Banu Khazraj, led by chiefs like Sa'd ibn Ubadah, welcomed the Prophet and became known as the Ansar (helpers), though some leaders like Abd-Allah ibn Ubaiy opposed him and were later labeled as hypocrites (munafiqun). The tribe included several important sub-clans, notably the Banu Najjar, who were the maternal relatives of Muhammad's grandfather Abdul-Muttalib and in whose territory Muhammad initially settled and built his mosque. The Banu Sa'ida clan hosted the famous Saqifah gathering after Muhammad's death where Abu Bakr was chosen as the first caliph.

During the early Islamic period, the Banu Khazraj provided many prominent companions including Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, Jabir ibn Abd Allah, Hassan ibn Thabit, Ubayy ibn Kab, Abd Allah ibn Rawahah, Anas ibn Malik, Muadh ibn Jabal, and others. They participated in major military campaigns and played crucial roles in Islamic expansion. The tribe's influence extended beyond Arabia - the Nusaybah family of Jerusalem, Custodians of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, are descendants of Banu Khazraj who arrived in Jerusalem with the 7th-century Islamic conquest.

Centuries later, the Nasrid dynasty of Granada (1238-1492) claimed descent from the Ansar tribes of Medina, including the Khazraj. In 1228, Ibn al-Ahmar gathered the remains of the Muslim population in Granada and established al-Mamlika al-Nasria, deriving legitimacy from their claimed Ansari lineage. The Nasrids ruled as the last Muslim kingdom in Spain until their surrender to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492, when Muhammad XII (Boabdil) surrendered Granada to the Catholic Monarchs.

data.history:
The Banu Nabhan (Aswadan) were a prominent sub-branch of the ancient Tayy confederation, one of the most influential Qahtanite Arab tribes of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the tribe traced its ancestry through the Qahtanite lineage to Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy), who was descended from Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan. Specifically, Nabhan was descended from Aswadan, who was a son of Amr ibn al-Ghawth ibn Julhumah. The Banu Nabhan emerged as one of the leading sub-branches of the Al al-Ghawth division of Tayy in northern Arabia, alongside their tribal cousins the Banu Thu'al. The tribe originally migrated from Yemen to the Jabal Tayy region (the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges) in northern Arabia during the late 2nd century CE, where they established themselves as influential pastoralists and warriors.

During the early Islamic period, the Banu Nabhan played a crucial role in the conversion of the Tayy confederation to Islam. In 630-631 CE, a delegation of fifteen Tayy chiefs led by Zayd al-Khayr of the Banu Nabhan converted to Islam and pledged allegiance to Prophet Muhammad in what became a pivotal moment for the tribe's Islamic identity. Muhammad was particularly impressed by Zayd al-Khayr, who died shortly after his conversion. However, the tribe's loyalty was tested during the Ridda Wars following Muhammad's death in 632 CE. Some members of the Banu Nabhan, including Zayd's son Muhalhil, initially joined the rebel Tulayha at Buzakha, though they were later persuaded to return to Islam through the diplomatic efforts of Adi ibn Hatim and other loyal Tayy leaders.

The Banu Nabhan continued to play significant roles throughout Islamic history as part of the broader Tayy confederation. In 1021 CE, the Banu Nabhan led by Hamad ibn Uday besieged a Khurasani pilgrim caravan at Fayd near Jabal Tayy, demonstrating their continued influence in the region. As part of the Tayy's extensive tribal network, the Banu Nabhan's influence extended across the Syrian Desert, Palestine, and northern Arabia during the medieval period. Like many specific tribal branches of the ancient confederations, the Banu Nabhan were eventually absorbed into larger tribal groupings over the centuries, with their descendants likely integrated into the broader Shammar and other Tayy successor tribes that continue to inhabit the traditional Jabal Tayy homeland.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about Banu Tamim Amr, the tribal branch specified in the existing data. Instead, the research focuses entirely on Amr ibn Ma'dikarib al-Zubaidi (died 642 CE), a legendary Arabian cavalry commander from the Zubaid clan of Yemen who belonged to the Madhhij tribe confederation - a completely different lineage. Amr ibn Ma'dikarib was a Qahtanite Yemeni warrior from the Asir region near Abha who converted to Islam under Prophet Muhammad and died fighting the Sassanids at the Battle of Nahavand in 642 CE. His descendants, including sub-branches like Jubur, Obaid and Al-Uqaydat, are found mainly in Iraq, Syria and Saudi Arabia today. The research also mentions other unrelated Amr figures including Amr ibn Luhay al-Khuza'i and Banu Amr bin Auf, but provides no specific historical details about the Banu Tamim Amr branch referenced in the existing data. The Banu Tamim Amr would theoretically be part of the Adnani Arab confederation based in the Najd region as a subdivision of the broader Banu Tamim tribal system, but the provided research contains no verification of their specific history, migrations, territorial control, or development as a distinct tribal entity.

The Beja peoples are a Cushitic-speaking confederation native to the Eastern Desert region between the Nile Valley and Red Sea coast, inhabiting areas from southeastern Egypt through eastern Sudan into northwestern Eritrea since ancient times. Originally known as Medjay in ancient Egyptian texts and later as Blemmyes in Roman times, they are not originally from the Arabian Peninsula but have established connections to Arab tribal networks through historical migrations, trade relationships, and intermarriage. The Beja confederation includes major subdivisions such as the Bishareen, Hedareb, Hadendoa, Amarar, Ababda, Beni Amer, Hallenga, Habab, Belin and Hamran, with the Ababda subtribe exemplifying the process of Arabization while maintaining Beja ethnic identity.

The Ababda, one of the most prominent Beja subtribes, demonstrate the complex relationship between Beja identity and Arab genealogical claims. With a population exceeding 250,000, the Ababda are considered an arabized Beja tribe who historically lived as nomads between the Nile and Red Sea, from Qena to Berber on the Nile and from Halayeb to Hurghada on the Red Sea. Most Ababda now speak Arabic and claim descent from Kawahla through Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a companion of Prophet Muhammad, while simultaneously maintaining their Beja ethnic identity. Other Beja groups like the Bishareen and Amarar also claim Kahili descent through their respective tribal ancestors.

Historically, the Beja underwent significant transformation through interaction with Arab tribes including Juhaynah, Mudar, and Rabi'a from the 9th century onward. The Ababda may have formed through an Arab-Beja alliance similar to the alliance between the Rabia and the Hedareb in the late ninth century. During the Turco-Egyptian conquest of Sudan, the Ababda provided camel support, and in return, the Egyptian government recognized their control of trade routes and allowed them to levy a 10% toll on goods passing through their lands. Despite Arab genealogical claims, there is no evidence pointing to an actual Arab origin of the Ababda, as they remain virtually indistinguishable from other Beja groups except through language.

Culturally, the Beja maintain distinct traditions including the preparation of Jabana (coffee with roasted beans and ginger) and traditional dances such as Shakreeb and Hosheeb. The Shakreeb involves men forming lines with musical accompaniment while dancers compete, and Hosheeb features men with swords and shields made from elephant or giraffe hide dancing to rhythmic singing and clapping. The Ababda dialect of Arabic represents a northern extension of Sudanese dialects, most closely related to that of the Shukria tribe, and is heavily influenced by the Beja language. As late as the second half of the 19th century, the Ababda remained bilingual in Arabic and a Beja language closely related to Bisharin.

folkLegends.title: Descent from Abad through Kahil
folkLegends.story: Ababda tribal origin narratives identify them as descendants of a common ancestor named Abad, who is said to be a descendant of Kahil. Kahil is claimed as the common ancestor of the Kawahla, and the Ababda assert that Kahil was a descendant of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a companion of the prophet Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Alternative Banu Hilal Origin
folkLegends.story: Claims of descent from Selman of the Banu Hilal have been recorded among Ababda in Shendi during the 19th century, presenting an alternative genealogical narrative to the Zubayr ibn al-Awwam lineage.
