data.history:
The Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر, singular Al Dhahiri) is a prominent tribal confederation of the United Arab Emirates whose traditional center of influence was the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain region. According to early tribal history documented in the research, the Dhawahir originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman before settling in the Buraimi region, though the exact timing and circumstances of this migration are not specified in the available sources. By the turn of the 20th century, the tribe had established significant territorial control, owning much of the cultivated land in Al Ain oasis (excluding Buraimi village itself) and numbering approximately 4,500 people.

The Dhawahir tribal structure consisted of three main subsections with distinct territorial divisions: the Daramikah, who populated Hili and Qattara; the Jawabir centered in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi. The tribe followed a semi-nomadic lifestyle, staying in their oasis villages during the summer date season and moving throughout the Trucial States in winter. They maintained large flocks of sheep, herded camels, and developed a specialized wood-burning industry supported by the oasis's effective falaj irrigation system, trading extensively in charcoal. The fertile woodlands of the oasis provided the foundation for this economic activity, distinguishing them from purely nomadic Bedouin tribes.

The Dhawahir developed an unusually strong and longstanding alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation, which was relatively uncommon among interior tribes who typically asserted their independence. This relationship proved crucial during various conflicts with external powers, including the Wahhabis, Sultan of Muscat, and Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr of Sharjah. In 1840, Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan formally took responsibility for the Dhawahir for the first time, alongside the Bani Yas and Manasir tribes. The Dhawahir provided military support to the Al Nahyan, particularly during Wahhabi incursions, helping Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan clear Buraimi Oasis of Wahhabi forces by 1850.

However, this alliance faced severe strain under Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great'), who systematically purchased date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir, fundamentally altering ownership patterns throughout the oasis. This policy triggered a major rebellion in 1877, resulting in a month-long conflict where Zayed prevailed and took two Dhawahir sheikhs hostage to guarantee tribal compliance. The tension escalated again in 1891 when Zayed, supported by Dubai forces of 30 horsemen and 300 camel riders, captured the main Dhawahir settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now Al Ain). He constructed a fort to establish his dominion and appointed Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hilal Al Dhahiri as his loyal headman, effectively consolidating Al Nahyan control over the oasis. The tribe also maintained complex relationships with the Na'im, described as a later wave of settlers in Buraimi, with whom they frequently disputed territorial control. Today, the Dhawahir maintain close relations with the UAE's ruling Al Nahyan family, exemplified by Sheikh Nasser bin Zayed Al Nahyan, whose mother Sheikha Amna bint Salah Al Badi belonged to the Bedouin branch of the Dhawahir tribe.
data.nameEtymology: The name Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر) appears to be connected to their original territory of Al Dhahirah in present-day Oman, though the research does not provide explicit etymological analysis of the tribal name.

data.history:
Al-Ga'al (الجعل) constitutes one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan. The tribe forms part of the Manasir confederation, a complex amalgamation that developed over centuries through voluntary association between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab migrants, and other tribal minorities. According to documented tribal genealogies, al-Ga'al belongs to the broader Sudanese riverine Arab tribal complex centered in the region known as Dar al-Manasir.

Like other Manasir clans, al-Ga'al adopted Islam and Arabic as their primary language while maintaining their distinct tribal identity within the larger Manasir confederation structure. The tribe's integration into this confederation provided them with established systems of mutual rights, obligations, and territorial claims against neighboring tribes, while preserving their specific clan identity. The Manasir confederation includes both riverain clans like al-Ga'al and Bedouin clans, with the riverain groups pursuing small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile.

The al-Ga'al tribe exists within a broader context of Sudanese riverine tribes including the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah. Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-Ga'al, should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish systems of mutual respect, rights and obligations, uniting themselves in territorial and resource claims against neighboring tribes.

Haḍramaut is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation that established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic kingdoms in southern Arabia, based in the Hadhramaut valley of what is now eastern Yemen. According to the research, the Himyarite Kingdom conquered Haḍramaut around 300 CE, indicating that the Hadhramaut kingdom was a significant independent polity that controlled substantial territory in the early centuries CE. The kingdom was later conquered by Qataban in the early 2nd century AD before ultimately falling under Himyarite control in the 4th century. The Haḍramaut region formed part of the broader South Arabian political landscape that included the kingdoms of Saba', Qataban, and Himyar, with these polities engaging in complex patterns of conquest and reconquest over several centuries. By 300 CE, when Himyar achieved final dominance over southern Arabia, Haḍramaut had been incorporated into this unified South Arabian state. The tribal confederation's territory encompassed the fertile Hadhramaut valley and surrounding areas of eastern Yemen, which provided agricultural resources and access to important trade routes. Following the Islamic conquests, descendants of the Haḍramaut tribes continued to play important roles in the broader Islamic world, particularly in trade and scholarship. The region's strategic location between the Arabian Peninsula and the Indian Ocean made it a crucial link in maritime trade networks connecting East Africa, India, and the Mediterranean world.

data.history:
The Hawamil are documented as one of the constituent sections of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, appearing in contemporary records as Al Hameli/Hawamil (الهوامل/الهاملي), with the family name Al Hameli representing them in modern UAE society. According to historical sources cited by British colonial administrators, the Hawamil were reported to have originally been part of the Al Ali tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation, though the specific circumstances and timing of this integration are not documented in available sources.

As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Hawamil participated in the tribal movement from the Najd region of central Arabia to the coastal regions of what is now the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Yas, including the Hawamil section, initially settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. Following the discovery of fresh water on Abu Dhabi island in 1793, the confederation migrated there, with the town of Abu Dhabi being established in 1795.

The Hawamil, as part of the Bani Yas confederation, experienced the demographic changes that affected the broader tribal group throughout the twentieth century. The total Bani Yas population, which Lorimer estimated at around 12,000 in the early 1900s, declined to approximately 8,000 by the early 1950s and further to 5,884 by the 1968 Abu Dhabi census. This decline was attributed to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors.

Contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi identify more than 20 sections as forming part of the confederation, with the Hawamil being recognized as one of these constituent groups. The historical accounts of the confederation's composition have varied among different observers, reflecting shifting political relationships as associated tribes sometimes sought full integration under the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, while changing regional dynamics at other times encouraged assertions of autonomy.

data.history:
The Juboor tribe is historically connected to the Jabrids (Banu Jabr), an Arab dynasty that ruled extensive territories in eastern Arabia from the early 15th to early 16th centuries. The Jabrids were of Banu Uqayl origin, an ancient Arab tribe belonging to the Banu Ka'b branch of the larger Banu 'Amir confederation. The most prominent Jabrid ruler was Ajwad ibn Zamil, who died in 1496 and was described by contemporaries as being 'of tribe of Banu Uqayl origin' and as 'the king of al-Ahsa and Qatif and the leader of the people of Najd.' The Jabrid dynasty was established when Ajwad's elder brother deposed and killed the last Jarwanid ruler in Qatif in the early 15th century.

At their height, the Jabrids controlled the entire Arabian coast on the Persian Gulf, including the islands of Bahrain, and regularly led expeditions into central Arabia and Oman. They had significant influence in Oman, appointing Ibadhi Imams and receiving annual tribute. Following Ajwad's death, his kingdom was divided among descendants, with Migrin ibn Zamil inheriting al-Hasa, Qatif, and Bahrain. The Jabrid state was known for its strong support of Maliki law and traditional Islamic science, with Sultan Ajwad founding many schools across Eastern Arabia and Oman, and patronizing Nabati poetry.

The Jabrid kingdom began to decline after Migrin ibn Zamil fell in battle in Bahrain in 1521 during a failed attempt to repel Portuguese invasion. The kingdom collapsed soon afterwards on the mainland, though one branch remained active in Oman for nearly three centuries. The ultimate fate of the Jabrids remains uncertain among historians. Some believe they migrated to Iraq, while others suggest they are identical with the 'Jubur' section of the Bani Khalid tribe, which eventually took control of the eastern Arabian region after the Jabrids.

In contemporary times, the Juboor maintain a presence in Iraq, with the tribe being among those present in the town of Sulaiman Bek in Saladin Governorate, alongside other Arab tribes including Al-Nuaim, Bani Az, Albu Salih, Albu Sabah, and Al-Bayat. This suggests the continuation of the tribal structure and identity in modern Iraq, consistent with the historical pattern of Jabrid/Juboor presence in the region.

data.history:
The Kordshuli (Kurdish: Kurdşûlî, literally meaning 'Kurdish villagers') is a nomadic and pastoral Kurdish tribe that settled in Fars province, Iran during the 19th century. Originally from traditional Kurdish territories in northwestern Iran, they migrated south into Fars province, establishing their main settlement at Khonjesht in what is now Eqlid County. The tribe speaks Laki, a Kurdish dialect, maintaining their linguistic identity despite their southern migration.

The Kordshuli experienced an unusual historical arrangement during their time in Fars province - they briefly became part of the Qashqai confederation, a predominantly Turkic tribal federation. This association represents a rare example of a Kurdish tribe integrating with a Turkic confederation, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal alliances in southern Iran. However, the tribe regained their independence in the beginning of the 20th century, reasserting their distinct Kurdish identity separate from the Qashqai.

Population surveys from the mid-20th century document the persistence of their nomadic lifestyle well into the modern era. In 1960, the Kordshuli numbered around 600 households, with most still maintaining nomadic practices. By the 1970s, their population had decreased to 353 households, of which 296 remained nomadic, indicating that approximately 84% of the tribe continued their traditional pastoralist way of life despite increasing sedentarization pressures.

Today, the Kordshuli maintain their presence in several villages within Fars province. Their main settlement remains Khonjesht, which serves as the capital of Khonjesht Rural District in Eqlid County. They also form the majority population in Cheshmeh Rana, another village in the same rural district. The tribe represents one of the Kurdish communities that successfully adapted their pastoralist traditions to the southern Iranian environment while maintaining their distinct cultural and linguistic identity separate from the predominantly Persian and Turkic populations of Fars province.

data.history:
The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage, part of the greater Qahtani tribal confederation through the genealogical line of Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan. Originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, they became known for their frequent travels and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. According to their tribal biography, when their population grew and their traditional areas in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow, tribal elders gathered to decide their future. They rejected the suggestion to conquer neighboring tribes, as many were fellow Harb clans with whom they shared kinship through their fifth grandfather, and instead chose migration as their path forward.

The Mahamid moved north through Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled for a period before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually reached and settled in the Karak region of Jordan, where their numbers were so substantial that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the region. There they formed alliances with local clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established what became known as the Emirate of Mahamid, with the Al-Amru clan providing the majority of rulers during this period. Their emirate lasted for an extended time, though some orientalist and historical sources suggest their rule was eventually opposed by different clans who united against them, allegedly due to harsh governance that prompted this resistance.

As part of the larger Harb tribal federation, the Mahamid are classified under the Banu Salim section, specifically within the Maymoon subsection, where they appear as 'Al-Mahamid (Al-Mihmadi)' in tribal genealogies. Today, the tribe has spread across multiple countries throughout the Arabian Peninsula, the Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa. In Sudan, they form part of the Northern Rizeigat confederation, with the Um Jalul clan exercising tribal leadership - notably through figures like Musa Hilal, whose Um Jalul clan has played a significant role in Sudanese tribal politics and the Darfur region.

The modern Mahamid maintain extensive geographical distribution with distinct branches: in Saudi Arabia they include the alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea clans; in Jordan they branched into Al Abdul Dayem, Al-Shaleikh, Al-Bahri, and Ayal Abdo, forming various tribal alliances with Al-Huwaitat, Bani Abbad, Bani Hamida, and Zoubi; in Palestine they include the kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad branches. They also maintain presence in Syria (where the Hourani branched from them), Kuwait, Iraq, and various Arab African countries, preserving their identity as part of the Harb tribal confederation while adapting to diverse political and geographical contexts across the Arab world.

folkLegends.title: The Tribal Council Migration Decision
folkLegends.story: When the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to take opinions about what they need to do. Some suggested conquering the neighboring tribes, but this suggestion was rejected because most of those in the neighborhood were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe, and they were all united by the fifth grandfather. Instead, they settled their opinion on leaving and migration.

folkLegends.title: The Journey to Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant
folkLegends.story: The last thing known of their cousins is that they went to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in the north, settled there for a period of time, and then moved towards the Levant until they reached the Karak region where they were of such a large number that they outnumbered the largest tribes in the region.

data.history: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that became traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe is believed to originally have migrated from Arabia, though the specific origins and timeframe of this migration are not documented in available sources. Upon settling in the Rams area, the Maraziq took the name of the district they inhabited, which had originally been populated by Iranians. The tribe established themselves in the western area of Rams, specifically in what became known as the Bani Humud neighbourhood or freej, located between Rams and Hulaylah. They shared this settlement area with another tribe, the Tunaij. The Maraziq lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses topped with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs, which were characteristic of the late Islamic township of Rams. By the early 1950s, the Maraziq population had left the Rams area entirely and became dispersed throughout the UAE. Notably, the Bani Humud area of Rams continued to be known as the Marzouqi neighbourhood even after the Maraziq families had departed, indicating their lasting association with the location.

data.history: The Mizahamiyin are a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation present in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, including the Mizahamiyin, are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin who remained nomadic, the Mizahamiyin had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a broader settlement pattern that saw most Bani Kaab subsections become sedentary. By 1844, the Bani Kaab tribe, including the Mizahamiyin, had allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a confederation that drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, allegiances in this region were notably fluid over time. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted their alignment to the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah confirming to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies, demonstrating their continued alignment with Saudi interests in this critical territorial dispute.

The Nahood are documented as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, appearing alongside other major clans including Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir. The Bani Khalid confederation traditionally claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, the companion of Muhammad and esteemed general, though Arab genealogists have questioned this claim and suggested alternative lineages including descent from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe. The chieftainship of the Bani Khalid has traditionally been held by the Al Humaid clan, not the Nahood.

As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, the Nahood participated in the rise to power in eastern Arabia during the 15th-18th centuries. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid clan in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's territory extended from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south, wielding significant political influence over Najd in central Arabia. The Bani Khalid maintained ties with tribal members who had settled in Nejd and cultivated clients among Najdi town rulers.

The Nahood, as part of the Bani Khalid confederation, became staunch enemies of the rising Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. When the emir of Uyayna adopted the ideas of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the Khalidi chief ordered him to cease support and expel Ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who subsequently moved to Dir'iyyah and joined forces with the Al Saud. The Bani Khalid attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion but failed. The Saudis eventually invaded the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa and deposed the ruling Al 'Ura'yir in 1789, though sources also mention 1793 as the date of conquest.

Following the Ottoman reinstatement of Bani Khalid rulers in 1818 after overthrowing the Al Saud, the confederation faced new challenges. The Bani Khalid were no longer the potent military force they once were, with tribes such as the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay' and Mutayr encroaching on their desert territories. Internal quarrels over leadership further weakened them. Despite forging an alliance with the 'Anizzah tribe, they were eventually defeated by tribal alliances along with the reestablished Al Saud. A decisive battle with an alliance led by the Mutayr and Ajman tribes in 1823, followed by another battle with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, ended Bani Khalid rule. Many members of the confederation, including likely Nahood families, subsequently dispersed to various regions including Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, and other parts of Saudi Arabia, where descendants continue to reside today.

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Desert Bustard
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

data.history:
Qays Aylan was a major Arab tribal confederation that branched from the Mudar group, with their progenitor traditionally identified as Qays ibn Aylan. According to Ibn Khaldun, 'Qays Aylan' was actually the epithet of al-Nas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them part of the Adnanite or 'North Arabian' lineage. The confederation comprised three main branches: Khasafa (including Hawazin, Sulaym, and Muharib), Sa'd (including Ghatafan with its Abs and Dhubyan sub-tribes, and A'sur with Ghani and Bahila), and Amr (including Adwan and Fahm). By the 7th century, the descendants of Qays were so numerous that the term Qaysi came to refer to all North Arabians.

Originally inhabiting the low-lying areas of the Tihamah along the western Arabian Hejaz, by Muhammad's time (ca. 570 CE) the Qaysi tribes had spread throughout central and eastern Arabia, parts of Lower Mesopotamia, and regions around Medina and Mecca. Initially hostile to Islam, tribes like the Ghatafan and Sulaym fought against the Muslims at Medina between 622-629. However, by 630 CE most had converted, though many apostatized during the Ridda Wars before rejoining Islam after their defeat at the Battle of Buzakha.

During the Umayyad period (661-750), the Qays formed one of the main political factions opposing the Yamani tribes led by Banu Kalb. This rivalry had both political and ethnic dimensions, as Qays represented 'northern Arabs' while Yaman were 'southern Arabs.' The Qays suffered a severe defeat at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, leading to years of revenge-driven conflicts. Under leaders like Zufar ibn al-Harith al-Kilabi, they resisted from strongholds in al-Qarqisiya and Ras al-Ayn until submitting to Umayyad authority in 691.

The Qays reached their political peak under Marwan II, who relocated the Umayyad capital to Harran in Qaysi territory. However, their power collapsed after the Battle of the Zab in 750, where they fought as Marwan's principal military force against the victorious Abbasids. Following this defeat, massive numbers of Qaysi tribesmen migrated to North Africa and al-Andalus, including the Banu Sulaym in Ifriqiya, Fazara in Cyrenaica, Banu Ashja' in Algeria and Morocco, and Banu Hilal in various North African regions. By the 14th century, only remnants remained in their central Arabian homeland.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Aylan Epithet
folkLegends.story: Various theories exist about the origins of the 'Aylan' part of Qays Aylan's name. Some traditions hold that Aylan was the name of al-Nas's famous horse, while others claim it was his dog, his bow, a mountain where he was born, or a man who raised him.

data.nameEtymology: The full name Qays Aylan derives from their progenitor Qays, with various disputed theories about 'Aylan' including it being the name of a horse, dog, bow, mountain, or foster father. Ibn Khaldun asserts 'Qays Aylan' was the epithet of al-Nas ibn Mudar.

The Riyah (Arabic: رياح) is one of the most powerful sub-tribes of Banu Hilal, tracing their genealogy through the Adnani lineage to Riyah, son of Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Originally from the Najd region of central Arabian Peninsula, the Riyah were among the major tribal divisions of Banu Hilal, alongside the Athbaj and Zughba. During the pre-Islamic period, their history was intertwined with other Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a tribes in the ayyam al-arab (battle days of the Arabs). The tribe likely accepted Islam after Muhammad's victory at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE, but did not participate in the Ridda Wars following the Prophet's death.

In the early 8th century, some Riyah migrated from Najd to Egypt, later moving to Iraq and the Levant in the 9th century. The most significant migration occurred in the 11th century when the Fatimid Caliphate dispatched the Banu Hilal confederation, including the Riyah, to punish the rebellious Zirid dynasty of Ifriqiya. At the time of this great migration, their chief was Munis ibn Yahya of the Mirdas family. The Riyah were part of the estimated 200,000 to 1 million Arab nomads who transformed North Africa's demographic and cultural landscape through this massive population movement.

By Ibn Khaldun's era (1332-1406), the Riyah had established themselves primarily in eastern Algeria around Constantine and northern Tunisia. The tribe was internally organized into several sub-fractions: Mohammed, Mirdass, Sinber, and Dhouaouda. The Riyah demonstrated significant military prowess throughout the medieval period, notably in 1217 when their warriors reinforced the Almohad army against the Marinids, killing their leader Abd al-Haqq I. Ibn Abi Zar recorded this victory and identified the Riyah Arabs as the strongest tribe in the Maghreb at that time.

However, the tribe's resistance to Almohad authority led to their devastating defeat at the Battle of Sétif on April 27, 1153. Under the leadership of Mahrez ibn Ziyad, a faction of the Riyah opposed Abd al-Mu'min's demands to participate in campaigns against Christian Spain. The battle resulted in Mahrez's death, his head displayed on a pike, and the subsequent dispersal of his tribal section. The Almohads captured 80,000 Riyah tents and relocated the survivors to Morocco, where they were resettled along with other Hilalian tribes like the Zughba and Khult. This forced relocation contributed to the rural Arabization of the western Maghreb. The Riyah played a crucial role in spreading Bedouin Arabic culture and nomadic lifestyle across North Africa, fundamentally altering the region's linguistic and cultural identity, with their descendants becoming integrated into various North African tribal structures.

data.history:
The Salihids (Arabic: بنو سليح, Banū Salīḥ) were a dominant Arab foederati tribe that served the Byzantine Empire in the 5th century CE, succeeding the Tanukhids and preceding the Ghassanids as Byzantium's chief Arab allies. According to genealogical sources, they stemmed from the Quda'a tribal confederation, with their lineage traced as Salīḥ ibn Ḥulwān ibn ʿImran ibn al-Ḥafī ibn Quḍāʿa. The tribe's royal house was known as the Zokomids (Ḍajaʿima), founded by Zokomos (Ḥamāṭa) who converted to Christianity along with his subjects after a monk prophesied the birth of his son. Originally concentrated in the Wadi Sirhan and Balqa regions of modern Jordan, they were charged with collecting taxes from Bedouins within the Limes Arabicus and protecting the Byzantine frontier from raiders.

The most notable Salihid phylarch was Dawud (David), a Christian king who initially engaged in military campaigns but later embraced religious life, earning the nickname 'al-Lathiq' (the bedraggled) for carrying water and mortar during the construction of his monastery, Dayr Dawud, between Resafa and Ithriya in northern Syria. His piety weakened his military position, leading to his death in battle at the hands of Tha'laba ibn Amir of Banu Kalb and Mu'awiya ibn Hujayr of Banu Namir between al-Qurnatayn and Mount Harib. The Salihids' decline accelerated after their participation in Emperor Leo I's failed 468 CE expedition against the Vandals in North Africa, which decimated their forces.

The tribe was ultimately defeated by the Ghassanids around 502 CE, becoming subordinate federate tribes. The killing of key Salihid leaders including Dawud, his cousin Ziyad (who died at the battle of al-Baradān against the Kinda), and tax collector Sabīṭ ibn al-Mundhir (slain by Ghassanid chief Jidʿ ibn ʿAmr) contributed to their downfall. During the Islamic conquest of the Levant in the 630s, Christian Salihids fought alongside other Arab federate tribes against Muslim forces at Dumat al-Jandal and Ziza, refusing conversion offers at Qinnasrin in 638. The tribe largely dispersed throughout Syria and Iraq, joining the Quda'a confederation during the Umayyad period, with their staunch Christianity rendering them isolated in the Islamic era unlike other Arab tribes whose members continued to flourish.

folkLegends.title: The Prophetic Birth of Zokomos' Son
folkLegends.story: According to Sozomen, Zokomos became a phylarch under the Byzantines and converted to Christianity along with 'all his subjects' after 'a certain monk of great celebrity' prophesied the birth of his son on condition of embracing the Christian faith. The son born was named Amr, whose name signifies a good omen, and he may have been the prophesied child who founded a branch of the Zokomid household.

data.nameEtymology: The actual name of the tribe's eponymous progenitor Salīḥ, according to the Jamharat, was ʿAmr

data.history:
The Shuraifat are a tribal group within the Al-Muntafiq confederation, specifically part of the Al Ajwad division. The Al-Muntafiq was a large Arab tribal confederation predominantly settled in southern Iraq's provinces and northern Kuwait, with most tribes tracing their genealogy to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and held control over the region of Basrah under Ottoman suzerainty. In 1521, they successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif (eastern Saudi Arabia) on the Ottomans' behalf, before being expelled by Banu Khalid.

During the Ottoman era, from the late eighteenth century onwards, most Al-Muntafiq tribes, including the Shuraifat, converted from Sunni to Shia Islam and transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. The Shuraifat participated in the seasonal nomadic migrations with the Albu Salih clans during the early-to-mid 20th century. These migrations involved moving south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn, traveling through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trade with local villages and towns, before returning north in spring to their tribal territories for summer.

By the 1940s-1950s, a majority of the Shuraifat nomads had left the nomadic lifestyle and settled in countries, cities, and towns that were either part of, or close to the indigenous lands of the Muntafiq confederation. The confederation as a whole is not homogeneous in terms of sect/religion, with centuries of intermarriage and intermingling creating a mix of Sunni and Shia tribes. Many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation, including members of the Shuraifat tribe.

The city of Nasiriya in southern Iraq was named after one of the Al-Muntafiq sheikhs, and the surrounding province was known as 'Al-Muntafiq Province' until 1976. The Al-Muntafiq confederation was divided into three major divisions: Bani Saeed, Bani Malik, and Ajwad, with the Shuraifat being listed among the Al Ajwad division tribes alongside Al Usoom, Al Shuwailat, Al Humaid, Al Rufai, Al Bdour, Al Juwarin, and Al Ghezzi.

data.history:
The Banu Taghlib were a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit, tracing their lineage through the Rabi'a confederation to the Adnanites. Originally inhabiting Najd, the tribe belonged to the larger Rabi'a branch alongside the tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Anizah. Their full genealogy follows: Taghlib ibn Wā'il ibn Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afṣā ibn Duʿmī ibn Jadīla ibn Asad ibn Rabīʿa ibn Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. The tribe was organized around the al-Araqim, descendants of six sons of Bakr ibn Hubayb ibn Amr ibn Ghanm, with the two largest divisions being Jusham and Malik, collectively called al-Rawkān ('the two horns'). The Hamdanid dynasty would later trace its descent to the Malik division via their ancestor Adi ibn Usama ibn Malik.

In the late 5th century, the Taghlibi chieftain Kulaib ibn Rabiah was murdered by his brother-in-law Jassas ibn Murra al-Shaybani of the Banu Bakr, precipitating the famous Basus War between the two cousin tribes. After their decisive defeat at the battle of Yawm al-Tahaluq, the bulk of Taghlib fled Najd for the Lower Euphrates region, later migrating further north to Upper Mesopotamia (Jazira) after their chieftain Amr ibn Kulthum assassinated the Lakhmid king Amr ibn al-Hind in 568 CE. The region of Diyar Bakr in southern Turkey takes its name from their presence alongside their cousins Banu Bakr.

The Taghlib embraced Miaphysite Christianity and remained largely Christian long after the advent of Islam. During the early Muslim conquests, sections initially fought against Muslim armies but eventually secured special treatment, negotiating to pay zakat rather than jizya while retaining their Christian faith. Under the Umayyads, they allied with the dynasty and achieved a decisive victory over rebellious Qaysi tribes at Yawm al-Hashshak. Their champion during this period was the court poet al-Akhtal, who engaged in 'verbal warfare' with the Qaysi poet Jarir.

During Abbasid rule, significant numbers converted to Islam in the 9th century, particularly through the persuasion of Malik ibn Tawk, the Taghlibi governor who founded the fortress town of al-Rahba. The tribe's political pinnacle came with the Hamdanid dynasty (880s-1002 CE), descendants of the Adi ibn Usama line, who established autonomous emirates in Mosul (under Nasir al-Dawla from 935) and Aleppo (under Sayf al-Dawla from 945). However, the Hamdanids relied on non-Arab ghilman and bureaucrats rather than their Taghlibi tribesmen for governance. After the early Hamdanid period, the Taghlib disappeared from historical records as a distinct tribal entity, having 'disappeared into obscurity' like many Arab tribes of the region.

data.history:
The Urayna was a historical Arabian tribe mentioned in early Islamic sources and classical Arab genealogical texts, traditionally associated with the central Arabian Peninsula region. According to traditional genealogical records, the Urayna was one of the clans within the broader Bajila tribal confederation, alongside other clans including Ahmas, Qasr, and Zayd ibn al-Ghawth. The Bajila confederation, which included Urayna as a subdivision, was identified in Arab genealogical tradition as either Qahtanite (southern Arabian) or Adnanite (northern Arabian) in origin, though the exact lineage remains uncertain among traditional genealogists.

The tribe gained particular historical significance during the early Islamic period through a controversial incident that profoundly influenced Islamic jurisprudence. According to early Islamic sources, members of the Urayna tribe feigned conversion to Islam with the intent to steal Muslims' possessions and killed a young shepherd who had been sent to teach them about the faith. This incident led to the revelation of Quranic verse 5:33, known as the Hirabah verse, which specified punishments for those who 'wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive to spread disorder in the land.' The original meanings of the triliteral root ḥrb, from which hirabah derives, include both despoiling someone's wealth or property and fighting or committing sinful acts.

During the early years of Islam, the Urayna clan became involved in the internecine fighting that led to the splintering and dispersal of the broader Bajila tribal confederation. Due to blood feuds with neighboring tribes and conflicts between various Bajila clans, including Urayna, the confederation became fragmented and many of its constituent clans became attached to larger tribal groups. Some Bajila clans joined the Banu 'Amir between Ta'if and Jabal Tuwayq, while others affiliated with the Jadila of Qays who inhabited the area south of Ta'if.

A notable historical connection to the Urayna occurred during the Second Muslim Civil War period (685-687 CE), when the Persian convert Abu Amra Kaysan became a mawla (client) of the Urayna clan within the Bajila tribe. Kaysan served as a prominent leader of the mawali faction in al-Mukhtar al-Thaqafi's pro-Alid movement, commanding al-Mukhtar's personal guard and overseeing punishments of Arab nobles in Kufa who had participated in events surrounding the killing of al-Husayn in 680 CE. The extinct Shia sect known as the Kaysanites is most likely named after this Abu Amra Kaysan, who had become affiliated with the Urayna clan. Like many smaller Arabian tribes of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, Urayna's historical presence is primarily documented through genealogical records and early Islamic historical accounts, with their current status in modern times being poorly documented.

The Zahairat are documented as a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe (Arabic: بني كعب), which has historical presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab are associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi in the UAE, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. The tribe consists of multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. By the turn of the 20th century, the Zahairat were among the settled sections of the Bani Kaab, as opposed to the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections, with a total tribal population of approximately 7,250 of whom 1,150 were Bedouin.

In 1844, the Bani Kaab, including the Zahairat, formed an alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation united to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance reflected the broader pattern of tribal coalitions in southeastern Arabia during the mid-19th century. Despite their alliance under the Bani Yas, the Bani Kaab maintained rivalries with the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, and had pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain.

The tribe's political alignments shifted significantly over the following century. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had allied with the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, 'Our territories are yours.' Interestingly, although the Na'im and Bani Kaab were frequently opposed to each other, they came together in the 1940s when oil concessions loomed, opposing the oil companies, the imposition of the rule of the Trucial Sheikhs, the Sultan of Muscat and the British alike.

During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Zahairat's parent tribe supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and were involved in fighting against the British force, the Trucial Oman Levies. This represented the culmination of their alliance with Saudi Arabia and their opposition to British influence in the region. The Zahairat, as part of the settled sections of the Bani Kaab, have maintained their presence in the UAE and Oman while adapting to the modern development of the Gulf states.

data.history:
The Banu Kalb (Arabic: بنو كلب, 'Sons of Kalb') was a major Arab tribe of Qahtani origin that emerged as a dominant force in northwestern Arabia and central Syria from the 4th century CE onwards. According to Arab genealogical tradition, the tribe descended from Kalb ibn Wabara, whose name means 'dog' in Arabic. The tribe was part of the larger Quda'a tribal confederation, with all branches descending from Rufayda ibn Thawr ibn Kalb, except for three small clans. The most prominent branch was the Banu Abdallah ibn Kinana, particularly the Banu Janab subbranch, which provided the tribe's chiefs and included the aristocratic Banu Haritha ibn Janab family.

In pre-Islamic times, the Kalb dominated the al-Jawf depression, including Dumat al-Jandal oasis, and controlled the vast Samawa desert between Syria and Mesopotamia. Under the legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab in the early 6th century, the tribe wielded significant influence among northern Arabian Bedouins, destroying the Ghatafan's rival sanctuary to the Ka'aba and conducting military campaigns for the Aksumite ruler Abraha against the Taghlib and Bakr tribes. As Byzantine foederati, the Kalb embraced Monophysite Christianity and were charged with guarding the empire's eastern frontier against Sassanian Persia and the Lakhmids.

The tribe's political prominence peaked during the early Islamic period through strategic alliances with the Umayyad dynasty. Key figures like al-Asbagh ibn Amr of Dumat al-Jandal established early pacts with Prophet Muhammad around 627-628, though most of the tribe remained Christian at Muhammad's death in 632. The Kalb's neutrality during the Muslim conquest of Syria (633-638) and subsequent conversion positioned them as crucial allies for the emerging Islamic state. Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan, the future Umayyad caliph, forged marital ties with the tribe, notably marrying Maysun, daughter of the preeminent chieftain Bahdal ibn Unayf.

These marriages became the foundation of Umayyad power, with the Kalb receiving significant privileges including consultation rights in major decisions and annual stipends for 2,000 nobles. The tribe's military support proved decisive at the Battle of Siffin (657) and later at the crucial Battle of Marj Rahit (684), where they defeated their main rivals, the Qays, establishing the long-running Yaman-Qays factional conflict. Under the Marwanid Umayyads, the Kalb remained influential until the pro-Qaysite Marwan II (744-750) marginalized them, leading to their gradual political decline under the Abbasid Caliphate.

After the mid-11th century, the Kalb's influence waned as they transitioned from nomadism to sedentarism, particularly in the Hauran region. With the exception of occasional mentions as allies of Turkish atabegs or caravan raiders, the Bedouin tribes largely disappeared from the political map by the end of the 11th century. The Kalb continued its shift to sedentarism into the 12th century, especially in the Hauran, and after this point, the tribe no longer appears consistently in the historical record as a major political force.

folkLegends.title: Zuhayr ibn Janab's Extraordinary Longevity
folkLegends.story: According to Arab legends, Zuhayr ibn Janab lived to 450 years of age, and his grandfather Hubal lived to 650. Zuhayr was recorded as one of the muʿammarūn (centenarians) and was noted in Arab fables for his extremely long life.

folkLegends.title: The Destruction of Ghatafan's Sacred Sanctuary
folkLegends.story: When the Ghatafan tribe constructed a haram (religious sanctuary) for their idols at a place called 'Buss' to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca, Zuhayr ibn Janab resolved to destroy it. He exclaimed 'By God, that will never happen while I am alive! I will never leave Ghatafan alone while they take a haram', then gathered his Kalbi tribesmen, defeated the Ghatafan and destroyed their sanctuary.
folkLegends.source: Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani and traditional Arab sources

folkLegends.title: Zuhayr's Death by Suicide
folkLegends.story: Zuhayr ibn Janab is said to have killed himself by drinking unmixed wine after being disobeyed by his followers.
